beats.prompt: Quick recap before the boxes — read the card, then continue.

interaction.title: Geometric wait and the harmonic sum
interaction.body: If each try succeeds with probability p, the expected number of tries is E = 1/p. Chaining N such waits with shrinking probabilities gives the harmonic sum H_N = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + … + 1/N. For N = 6: H₆ = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + 1/5 + 1/6 = 49/20 = 2.45.

feedback.correct: E = 1/p — the building block for every stage ahead.
hints: H_N is the sum of 1/k for k = 1 to N.

beats.prompt: Before the boxes, match each random wait to its expected count.

pairs.left: Each try succeeds with prob p
One face of a fair die (p = 1/6)
Next new type (k of N held)

feedback.correct: Good — each geometric wait costs 1/p; a full set chains several such waits.
hints: Tap a description, then its expected wait.
p = 1/6 gives E = 6; p = 1/2 gives E = 2.
E = N/(N−k) is the per-stage formula when k types are held.

beats.prompt: You want the full set of 6 toy types. Each cereal box is equally likely to hold any one of the 6. How many boxes do you expect to buy?

Just over 6.note: Let's test it — the first toys come fast, but the last ones drag, more than doubling the total.

About 8.note: Let's test it — closer, but the rare final toys push it higher.

Around 12–15.note: Good instinct — let's prove it by summing the ever-longer waits for each new toy.

beats.prompt: You've found 5 of the 6 toy types. Only one is still missing, and it appears in 1 of every 6 boxes. How many more boxes do you expect to buy?

fields.label: Expected boxes for the last type

feedback.correct: E = 1/p = 1/(1/6) = 6 — the last type is the slowest, averaging 6 boxes alone.
hints: Only 1 of 6 boxes now helps, so the last type isn't quick: E = 1/p = 6.

beats.prompt: Tap 'Draw box' to open cereal boxes until you've collected all 6 toy types. Watch the running expected total Σ N/(N−i+1) build, and notice how the per-stage hit-probability (N−k)/N shrinks as you collect.

hero.structuralReadout: On average, the full set of 6 takes 147/10 = 14.7 boxes — the running Σ N/(N−i+1) converges to N·H₆.

feedback.correct: Set complete — it took far more than 6 boxes, and the last type alone cost the most.
hints: Keep drawing until all 6 toy types appear.
Watch how the last type stalls the running total.

beats.interviewNote: N·H_N ~ N·ln N for large N (the integral approximation of the harmonic series). The rare last type alone costs N boxes — that single stage explains why total cost grows faster than linear; doubling N more than doubles the expected boxes. For N = 6: 14.7 boxes; for N = 52 (a full card deck): ≈ 236.
beats.prompt: Each stage i is a geometric wait with success probability p = (N−i+1)/N, so it costs N/(N−i+1) boxes on average. By linearity of expectation (L2), E[full set] = Σ_{i=1}^{N} N/(N−i+1) = N·H_N.

interaction.title: E[full set] = N·H_N
interaction.body:
Six geometric waits, each longer than the last:
  Stage 1: 6/6 = 1  (first type; p=1)
  Stage 2: 6/5 = 1.2
  Stage 3: 6/4 = 1.5
  Stage 4: 6/3 = 2
  Stage 5: 6/2 = 3
  Stage 6: 6/1 = 6  ← the slow one
Sum = 14.7 = 6·H₆ = N·H_N. Linearity (L2) lets us add these waits without worrying about dependence between stages.

feedback.correct: E[full set] = N·H_N — linearity threads six geometric waits into one total.
hints: Each stage i has hit-prob (N−i+1)/N and wait N/(N−i+1).
Sum six such waits using linearity.

beats.prompt: Counting step — read, then try the next problem.

interaction.title: Count the new types
interaction.body: When you hold k of N types, exactly (N−k) of the N possible box outcomes show a new type. So: p = (N−k)/N, and E = 1/p = N/(N−k). Example: k = 2, N = 6 → (N−k) = 4 new types out of 6 → p = 4/6 = 2/3 → E = 3/2.

feedback.correct: p = (N−k)/N — count the new types, not 1/N.
hints: N−k of the N box outcomes are new.

beats.prompt: You hold 2 of the 6 toy types. A box shows a new type if it holds one of the 4 types you don't have yet. What's the expected number of boxes until your next new toy?

fields.label: Expected boxes for next new type

feedback.correct: p = 4/6 = 2/3, so E = 1/p = 3/2. The hit-prob is (N−k)/N, falling as you collect.
hints: With 2 types held, 4 of 6 boxes are new, so p = 4/6 and E = 3/2.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: you buy boxes until you hold the full set of N = 6 toy types. Sum the six geometric waits. Give the expected total as an exact fraction.

interaction.scenario: Each new type costs \(\tfrac{N}{N-k}\) boxes on average. Sum all six stages — but remember, the last few are expensive.

feedback.correct: The coupon-collector total is the sum of geometric waits: \[ E = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \frac{N}{N-k} = N\,H_N \] With \( N=6 \): \( H_6 = 1+\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{4}+\tfrac{1}{5}+\tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac{49}{20} \), so \( E = 6\cdot\tfrac{49}{20} = \tfrac{147}{10} = 14.7 \). The last few types dominate — growth is \( N\ln N \), not linear.
hints: Cost grows like \(N\ln N\), not linearly. Sum the six waits: \(6 \cdot H_6 = 6 \cdot \tfrac{49}{20} = \tfrac{147}{10}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap cards to lock in the collection formula.

feedback.correct: A full set = sum of ever-longer geometric waits; N·H_N sets up the extremes ahead in L6.
hints: Each new type is a geometric wait with shrinking hit-probability.
